transcript:
How's it going everybody? Uh, thanks for coming. I loved presenting at my fifth Breakpoint here. It's been a long time coming, and we're really changing the space now. Uh, my name is Doctor Blocks—we'll go with Doctor Blocks—and I'm CTO of Switchboard. And we've been an oracle provider for Solana for about five years now. And, you know, in 2020, and 2021, 2022, uh, there was a very different model for how to connect information to blockchains. And we'll get to how this has changed over the years now.

So, really when we started Switchboard, there was only, um, a single method that people really, uh, used to push information from off-chain, uh, to on-chain. And when you think about why you need, uh, oracles, it's really because you have a large source of information that's actually off-chain, that is actually from the internet. And when you think about information from the internet versus information from on-chain, there's actually not a clear way to come to consensus on what that information is. If I am pulling some data from a website, how can I actually verify what this is and this price is honest and true, and actually a strong and legitimate price.

Uh, so what we build is something called oracles, which is a consensus mechanism for information. This can be information from the internet, information from actually first-party sources that actually produce this information. But Switchboard actually turned this a bit to be a platform to build your own oracles, and you can actually make a layout of how you push and transform this information on the blockchain. So Switchboard is actually the first oracle platform where you can build your own information workflows and sources.

So with that, we actually launched our Push Oracle in 2021, but they've been around since 2017 with the advent of Chainlink or so. And the first two years, we were doing between 3 to 7% of Solana's traffic. This of course, pushing this data constantly even when it's not used, is highly inefficient and unsustainable. And with that, we actually had this advent of what we call a Pull Oracle, which you pull this information on-chain when the user actually needs it. What we're coming to now is Just-In-Time oracles. And when I actually talk about this, we're talking about this whole execution environment for when you only pull information when somebody has already used it. So you don't actually have this flow of needing to constantly update this information or even letting the users, uh, be subject to choosing the ordering.

So, yeah. In the Push Model Era, 2017 to 2022. This was very expensive because a lot of this was before the advent of Solana. Some of this, and most of this, was actually in the EVM/Ethereum days. In which Chainlink was trying to push information for a lot of, uh, you know, uh, first-party assets on-chain, but because it's so expensive, it could be hundreds of dollars of gas fees a day, maybe even thousands, uh, they actually had a heartbeat interval of 30 minutes. This is fine for things like lending, because lending is actually over-collateralized. And if you can actually make a system that you can check the volatility of how far things can actually manage to be pushed in a 30-minute period, and it's over-collateralized, there's actually limited risk to doing this. So when you have oracles and it's actually at this cadence, you can do it for certain types of DeFi, but it's focused on lending and we don't get to any many more derivatives other than maybe, uh, started the advent of early perps. And there are applications like GMX that found ways around this. And when oracles started to speed up, we started seeing updates per minute, GMX actually implied a system of they'll take the worst price before and after a trade. Making sure that or—um, people executing trades could not exactly front-run the oracle's price.

Then we get to the Pull Model where we actually delegate the responsibility of pushing the information of oracles to the user. And in this way, it's much more practical for, uh, oracles to actually operate because actually even on Solana, there was getting to the point that some oracle, uh, uh, platforms were stipending their oracles $500,000 a month just to keep information up to date. Unsustainable for any or—uh, platform that's pushing information. Uh, and also if nobody's using the information between requests, it's, uh, wasted money and wasted blockspace. So with this Pull Model, it allows these users to actually request the price be updated only when they want to use it. And there's actually an off-chain consensus layer for where all this information goes and when it's ready to be pulled on, the user pulls it on.

Uh, beyond that, uh, beyond having just a consensus layer, since Switchboard is a permissionless platform, we actually employed this new model that we called an On-Demand Oracle. Where the information isn't only just brought from another layer onto the blockchain that you want at that specific time, but the oracles actually do the work to go fetch the information right when the user is requesting it. And with this, it becomes a lot more practical to start running some of these more high-fidelity applications. So if something like a perp DEX, you can actually start doing this at, you know, sub-5 seconds, uh, for the whole flow to get this information. But really, this is still not competitive with centralized, uh, perpetuals or centralized, uh, uh, DEXs even. So, or a—spot prices, excuse me. Uh, so when you're looking at these future platforms, when you look at actually competitive or you could say prop perpetuals that we're going to get to soon, or, uh, prop AMMs for spot prices, there's a whole different flow you have to employ. But this really did open up the bar for having something that's, uh, pretty competitive for a use case for, uh, per—perpetuals on-chain. Or even margined, uh, spot.

So now we're getting to this whole year where everyone's talking about Prop AMMs. It's Prop AMMs everywhere and they're taking over all the spot traffic. So what actually is a Prop AMM? You know, for some, uh, context here, many of you are going to know exactly what an AMM is. You have, uh, basically a book—it's not an order book—but it's basically a market that has two, uh, uh, treasuries of assets. One in a base token, one in a quote token. And you can actually get the price of these tokens based off—based off of the curve or the levels of these pools. And as one side of the pool depletes, the other increases, this changes the price. This still is not great for price discovery since it's algorithmic, and not actually what a market maker is willing to quote for all of these services. So when people actually deploy liquidity, it is still mostly off-chain.

Now we're getting to this point where we have Prop AMMs where people are saying constantly what they're willing to quote, and they're actually pushing their own oracle updates every single slot. Although this is very expensive, we've come to the point where oracle updates are extremely cheap. We've optimized this down to, uh, 20 or so compute units on-chain. And if you actually push this every slot, you can get up to about 70,000 to 120,000 compute units per asset. And if you can actually expand on this to, um, more compressed updates, you can get a little more advantageous. But even so, with this, we're seeing a lot, a lot of updates. And if you're actually going to be expanding to all the Prop AMMs coming up, this is millions and millions an epoch that are going to be spent just to support a few Prop AMMs. So is this really sustainable as we're going forward for Solana if everyone's going to have a fair—fair and balanced chance to actually make these applications?

So, now we talk about how we're limited in, uh, oracle updates. When you're actually submitting an oracle update for these Prop AMMs, they're trying to squeeze these prices and price updates as tight as possible. If you're trading on the New York Stock Exchange, they're talking about 50 microseconds for price updates and a change. If Solana or any decentralized system is going to compete with that, we need to be coming to that as our, uh, golden bar or golden standard. So we come to this one, uh, one slot problem. In which we have a single leader that has multiple oracle updates they're seeing. Why would they not be motivated just to drop all the other transactions that are in a, uh, less than beneficial spot for them to execute their own trades? Why would they just not use the—the single best price update that they get? So when you're actually getting, uh, updates from a Prop AMM, it's not incentivized for them to do more than one oracle update per slot. Because these validators can basically free-form—uh, free-formly drop whatever oracle updates they do not like.

So enter Jito BAM. Now we have actual marketplace for ordering transactions. This is going to be coming very soon to Solana. Jito is just finishing up, uh, their execution of this, and I think it's going to be a very big revolution of how we order blocks and how we can verifiably order blocks to prevent sandwiching. Um, now if you can actually, uh, uh, verifiably order blocks, you can have multiple updates in a slot. So this is intra-slot price updates. So even though, uh, Solana slots are 200 milliseconds, we can actually do price—price updates, um, at any point in the block. Now we can actually get to the microsecond level of pricing and trading. So when you have these solutions like Double Zero that have actually really optimized the networking layer between all of the validators, we're really seeing the benefits of this coming to fruition. Because now we no longer care about the, uh, distance between when slots finish. If you can get a price update before the oracle, um, uh, be—before the next oracle update actually finishes, it's still very, very meaningful for you.

Uh, we're running out of time, so I'm going to speed through the rest of these. But anyways, when Switchboard is created, we're enabled to make all these new mechanisms for people to push these prices. If you want to get the NAV price of your own treasuries, you can do that now. And that's exactly what Kiel did for instantiating their 2.5 billion, uh, dollar fund for MakerDAO on Solana. They've actually used Switchboard to, uh, uh, get their NAV price. Uh, you use this for prediction markets, just how Jupiter is using this with the Kalshi data on Solana. And now we're doing this for Prop AMMs. And with Switchboard actually doing their own, uh, BAM plugin, now people can free-formly, um, add their own oracle updates into the BAM layer to not spam the network and only put oracle transactions right before trades actually execute. And do this verifiably to not, uh, waste their own money. Uh, so this is kind of the summary of this bespoke tactics that BAM will actually employ for oracle updates.

And really this whole Block Auction Mechanism employs a whole new floor for actually ordering and pricing how much these updates are actually worth in value for making these trades. Um, yeah, summary there. Key takeaways here. The evolution of oracles has gone from a push-pull to back to a very efficient push but oracle or, uh, program-based. And now with Jito BAM, we can make a platform for people actually executing oracle updates just before trades. So be as efficient as possible, as timely as possible. Uh, yeah. Now it's really interesting now because with the advent of Prop AMMs, price discovery is for the first time ever moving on-chain rather than off-chain. And now it's very interesting because now people that are quoting prices might look on-chain or on Solana first before even a CEX.

If any questions here, please message me. I am Doctor Blocks on Telegram or SwitchboardXYZ. And please look at our docs and message if any questions. Thank you so much.

enrichment.generated_query: Just-In-Time oracles Switchboard Solana Breakpoint 2025 official announcement blog OR technical docs

sources.title: Introducing Drift v3: Built to Outperform  | Drift Updates
sources.snippet:
Introducing Drift v3: Built to Outperform  | Drift Updates
[![](https://cdn.prod.website-files.com/6310e7dee49f0866da8eed4c/6867a1f64ca2c75f0d14f371_16c97ce9017b907297827c5113a068f9_Frame%201597880158.avif)](https://www.drift.trade/)
[
Launch App
](#)
## Introducing Drift v3: Built to Outperform
December 4, 2025
![](https://cdn.prod.website-files.com/6310e7dee49f08e5798eed50/693023e13e4b72ada17e0fe2_2025-12-03%2017.19.13.jpg)
[
Contents
](#)
[
Contents
](#)
[
](https://twitter.com/driftprotocol)[
](https://discord.com/invite/fMcZBH8ErM)
[
Trade Now
](https://app.drift.trade/)
![]()
![]()
Drift v3 is the protocol’s biggest performance upgrade yet. Rebuilt around trader execution, v3 delivers**10x faster fills, 10x improved liquidity, and a cleaner, more intuitive interface**than v2.
This upgrade was built for one thing: outperformance. Instant execution. Deeper liquidity. A suite of tools that helps you trade faster and with more confidence.
Drift v2 was the first to bring cross-margin and multi-asset collateral to DeFi on-chain trading. We built on Solana to deliver NASDAQ-level performance on-chain and with major Solana upgrades like Alpenglow, Firedancer and Jito’s Block Assembly Market (BAM), Drift is built to outperform as the network scales.
Since launch, Drift has surpassed 1.5B in TVL, $133B+ in total trading volume and $650M in open interest. Drift v3 is built to take those numbers and your trading even further.
**Drift v3 will be available on Dec 4, 2025.**New users will need an invite code to access Drift v3.
**DM**[**@DriftProtocol**](https://x.com/DriftProtocol)**to request access.**
## Why v3?
Drift v3 represents the protocol’s next stage of maturity: the point where years of infrastructure, liquidity, and execution improvements converge into a faster, clearer, more reliable trading experience.
**Over the past year, Drift has evolved in three critical areas:**
1. Execution speed
2. Liquidity depth
3. Trader experience## How Drift v3 outperforms
*The road to v3 was focused on improving liquidity, trading execution and speed. Here’s what that looks like in practice:*
### **Trading Execution and Speed**
* **10x faster order fills:**On Drift v3, 85% of market orders are filled within the same slot. A slot is Solana’s smallest time unit (about 400 milliseconds). On Drift, that means that trades are executed in under half a second. Instant execution.
* **15x faster take profit/stop loss triggers:**For BTC, it dropped from 15 slots (\~6 seconds) to 1 slot (\~0.4 seconds). This means your risk controls and exits now trigger immediately, reducing slippage and missed opportunities.
* **Gasless trading by default:**Unlike other DEXs, where you pay gas on every trade, Drift abstracts gas fees so that’s one less thing to worry about eating into profits
* **400ms oracle updates**: Prices update every 400 millisecond (roughly 2.5 times per second). This keeps quotes accurate and reduces the chance of stale pricing.
* **Scales with Solana:**As Solana increases block size and throughput, Drift automatically gets faster too, handling more trades per second.### **Liquidity**
* **10x reduction in slippage on market orders:**In Drift v2, large market orders could move the price by 20 bps. In v3, that’s been cut to just 2 bps. Traders now get far better pricing, especially on size.
* **5x tighter TP/SL execution:**Take-profit and stop-loss orders are far more precise. On major markets like SOL and BTC, fills are 4x tighter, while non-majors see up to 5x improvement. This means exits trigger closer to the price you set, reducing losses and protecting gains.
* **Introduction of Drift Liquidity Provider (DLP)**: Provides liquidity for both Perps and Spot markets. Community members can deposit funds into DLP to support market-making and earn a share of the pool's profits. Currently in the testing stage. Public launch in Q1 2026.
* **Expanded Market Maker Program:**With v3’s expanded market maker initiatives, long-tail altcoins now hav

# Chainlink Runtime Environment Now Live, Unlocking the Full Capabilities of Onchain Finance
November 4, 202517 min read
Author:[Chainlink](https://blog.chain.link/author/chainlink/)
**Table of ContentsChainlink Runtime Environment Now Live, Unlocking the Full Capabilities of Onchain FinanceSmart Contracts Are Becoming Increasingly More AdvancedCRE: The Orchestration Layer of Onchain FinanceInstitutional Use Cases Being Built on CRE TodayDevelopers Can Get Started In MinutesThe Next Chapter for CRE and Onchain FinanceStart Building With CRE Today
[](javascript:void(0))
[![Facebook](https://blog.chain.link/wp-content/uploads/2021/03/icon-facebook.svg)]()[![Twitter](https://blog.chain.link/wp-content/uploads/2021/03/icon-twitter.svg)]()[![LinkedIn](https://blog.chain.link/wp-content/uploads/2021/03/icon-linkedin.svg)]()

[Subscribe to newsletter](https://pages.chain.link/subscribe?utm_medium=referral&utm_source=chainlink-blog&utm_content=post-sidebar)
We’re excited to announce the launch of the[Chainlink Runtime Environment (CRE)](https://chain.link/chainlink-runtime-environment), the all-in-one orchestration layer unlocking institutional-grade smart contracts for onchain finance.
[Start building](https://cre.chain.link/signup)
Prior to the launch of the Ethereum Virtual Machine (EVM), smart contract development was highly complex given the limited programmability of blockchains. In 2015, the EVM transformed blockchains by introducing a programmable execution environment for smart contrac

tags.impact_reason: Switchboard's evolution to Just-In-Time oracles is a solid update that improves efficiency and reduces costs for data on Solana, benefiting DeFi applications.

visual_idea.reasoning: I chose a futuristic, high-tech telephone switchboard as the Semantic Anchor because it directly visualizes the company's name, 'Switchboard,' and its function as an oracle connecting countless off-chain data sources to the on-chain world. The concept of plugging and unplugging cables represents the dynamic flow of information and consensus. For the text, I integrated '50µs' as a holographic detail on the machinery, highlighting the key performance metric—50-microsecond latency—mentioned in the standfirst, which is their competitive edge against the NYSE.
visual_idea.description: A vast, futuristic command center dominated by a monumental, floor-to-ceiling data switchboard made of polished chrome and dark metal. Thousands of glowing fiber optic cables, representing real-time price feeds, snake across its surface, being dynamically plugged into ports by robotic arms at imperceptible speeds. In the background, the server room extends into an infinite, hazy depth. A holographic display, projected diegetically from a console on the switchboard, glows with the metric '50µs', signifying the system's incredible low-latency performance.

final_prompt.prompt_text: A monumental, floor-to-ceiling data switchboard, crafted from gleaming polished chrome and deep dark metal, dominates a vast, futuristic command center. Thousands of vibrant, glowing fiber optic cables, visually representing real-time price feeds, snake intricately across its surface, their brilliant light creating a mesmerizing data-nexus. Robotic arms, moving with imperceptible speed, dynamically plug these illuminated cables into ports, creating trails of light. In the background, the server room stretches into an infinite, hazy depth, bathed in atmospheric volumetric lighting. A holographic display, projected diegetically from a console seamlessly integrated into the switchboard, radiates a soft glow with the metric '50µs', physically signifying the system's incredible low-latency performance. The composition is vertical (3:4), emphasizing the epic height and scale of the switchboard, with ample negative space above, making the structure feel truly monumental. Ultra-photorealistic, high dynamic range, cinematic lighting with dramatic contrast, realistic reflections on polished chrome surfaces, soft blended shadows from the glowing elements. 8k, octane render, raytracing, hyper-detailed.

visual_idea.reasoning: I chose a futuristic, high-tech telephone switchboard as the Semantic Anchor because it directly visualizes the company's name, 'Switchboard,' and its function as an oracle connecting countless off-chain data sources to the on-chain world. The concept of plugging and unplugging cables represents the dynamic flow of information and consensus. For the text, I integrated '50µs' as a holographic detail on the machinery, highlighting the key performance metric—50-microsecond latency—mentioned in the standfirst, which is their competitive edge against the NYSE.
visual_idea.description: An extreme macro shot focusing on a single port of the futuristic switchboard. A vibrant, glowing fiber optic cable clicks into a perfectly machined chrome socket. The connection point emits a brilliant pulse of light, creating a lens flare and illuminating the intricate, polished metallic textures and micro-engravings surrounding the port, symbolizing a single, precise data point being delivered at immense speed.

final_prompt.prompt_text: An extreme macro shot, 1:1 square aspect ratio, centered and tightly framed, filling the entire canvas with intricate details. The focus is on a single port of a futuristic switchboard. A vibrant, neon-glowing fiber optic cable, emitting a strong internal light, is precisely clicked into a perfectly machined, highly reflective polished chrome socket. The connection point bursts with a brilliant, luminous pulse of light, generating a dramatic anamorphic lens flare that radiates outwards. This intense light vividly illuminates the surrounding intricate, polished metallic textures and ultra-fine micro-engravings on the switchboard, highlighting every minute detail. Volumetric lighting emphasizes the glowing effect and the depth of the light pulse, creating an atmosphere of immense speed and precision data delivery, embodying a data-nexus aesthetic. The polished chrome surfaces reflect the light with crisp, photorealistic accuracy. 8k, octane render, raytracing, hyper-detailed, photorealistic, cinematic lighting, ultra-resolution, studio lighting.

editorial.headline: Switchboard Moves Global Price Discovery On-Chain via Jito BAM
editorial.standfirst: By leveraging Jito's Block Auction Market for intra-slot updates, Switchboard enables decentralized exchanges to rival the 50-microsecond latency standards of the NYSE. This architectural shift signals a turning point where primary price discovery occurs on Solana rather than centralized venues.
key_takeaways: Kiel utilizes Switchboard to stream real-time Net Asset Value (NAV) for its $2.5 billion MakerDAO fund on Solana.
The integration with Jito BAM allows for 'Just-In-Time' oracle updates, enabling microsecond-level precision by ordering price data immediately before trade execution.
Jupiter has deployed Switchboard infrastructure to ingest institutional data from Kalshi to power its prediction markets.
editorial.article_body_markdown:
The era of the 'Push' oracle model is effectively over, rendered obsolete by the demands of high-frequency on-chain trading. **Switchboard** has unveiled a critical evolution in oracle architecture designed to support the explosion of Proprietary Automated Market Makers (Prop AMMs) on Solana. While Prop AMMs provide tighter spreads, they historically burdened the network by spamming price updates every slot. The new paradigm leverages **Jito's Block Auction Market (BAM)** to introduce verifiable, intra-slot ordering. This allows market makers to insert oracle updates into a block *precisely* before a trade executes, eliminating the latency gap that previously separated DeFi from traditional finance.

This technical leap addresses a fundamental inefficiency in decentralized market structure. Previously, validators could arbitrarily drop oracle transactions, forcing market makers to over-broadcast updates to ensure inclusion. By utilizing **Jito BAM**, **Switchboard** enables a 'Just-In-Time' (JIT) execution environment. This reduces network congestion while simultaneously increasing data fidelity. The implication is profound: Solana is now targeting the 50-microsecond update frequency standard set by the New York Stock Exchange, a metric previously thought unattainable for decentralized ledgers.

From an institutional perspective, the most significant consequence is the migration of price discovery. For the last decade, on-chain prices were merely lagging derivatives of Binance or Coinbase data. With **Switchboard** facilitating microsecond-level updates via **Jito**, liquidity providers are beginning to quote prices on Solana *first*. This shift is already being utilized by major entities, including **Kiel**, which streams NAV data for a **$2.5 billion** fund, and **Jupiter**, which is routing **Kalshi** data for prediction markets. We are witnessing the transition of Solana from a settlement layer to a primary venue for global asset pricing.
